enrichment.short_description: Passbolt is an open-source password manager built for teams, offering encrypted credential sharing, self-hosted or cloud deployment, and AGPL-licensed source code developed by a Luxembourg-based company.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community edition); Pro from EUR 4.50/user/month billed annually
enrichment.seo_title: Passbolt Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Passbolt review 2026: open-source team password manager pricing, self-hosted vs cloud options, security features, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Passbolt is an open-source password manager designed specifically for teams and organizations, built on the OpenPGP standard and distributed under the AGPLv3 license. The project was started in 2014 by co-founders Kevin Muller and Cedric Alfonsi and the company was later established in Luxembourg, where Passbolt SA is headquartered in Belvaux; the business has received financial backing tied to the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg and closed an 8 million dollar Series A round in January 2025 to expand its password-collaboration and access-management capabilities. Passbolt's core differentiator versus mainstream consumer password managers like 1Password or Dashlane is its open-source, self-hostable architecture: organizations, particularly software development and DevOps teams, can run Passbolt on their own infrastructure for full control over encryption keys and data residency, or use Passbolt's own cloud-hosted version for convenience. The product centers on encrypted password storage and sharing, private and shared folders, user and group-based role access control, tagging, and an audit log of account activity, with browser extensions and desktop/mobile clients supporting the workflow. Pricing follows a freemium, self-hosted-first model: the Community edition is free forever with unlimited users and covers core password management, sharing, and two-factor secret-key authentication; the Pro edition costs EUR 4.50 per user per month billed annually (with a 10-user minimum and volume discounts above 100 users) and adds single sign-on via Microsoft, Google, or OpenID, LDAP and Active Directory provisioning, account recovery, and next-business-day support; an Enterprise edition adds custom development, high-availability and disaster-recovery consulting, and phone support under a 4-hour SLA. Special discounted terms are available for nonprofits, educational institutions, and open-source projects. Passbolt's target users are engineering-led organizations, agile and DevOps teams, and privacy- or compliance-conscious companies (including those in the EU seeking data sovereignty) that want a transparent, auditable alternative to closed-source enterprise password managers. Its main competitors include 1Password Business, Bitwarden (another open-source option), Dashlane Business, and Keeper Security, with Bitwarden being the closest comparison given its similarly open-source, self-hostable model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Passbolt is an open-source password manager purpose-built for teams rather than individuals, letting organizations centralize, organize, and securely share credentials across departments and projects. The software is built on the OpenPGP encryption standard and is released under the AGPLv3 license, meaning its full source code is publicly auditable, a point of differentiation from closed-source competitors that ask customers to trust their security claims without independent verification.

The company behind Passbolt is headquartered in Belvaux, Luxembourg, and positions itself explicitly as a European, GDPR-aligned alternative to US-based password managers. Passbolt can be self-hosted on a customer's own servers for maximum control over data residency and encryption keys, or run on Passbolt's own cloud infrastructure for teams that prefer a managed service without giving up the open-source codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Passbolt's core feature set covers encrypted password storage and one-click sharing, private and shared folders for organizing credentials by team or project, and group-based role access control so admins can grant or revoke access without exposing the underlying secret. Two-factor secret-key authentication protects account access, and an activity log records changes for audit and compliance purposes.

Paid tiers add enterprise directory and identity integrations, including single sign-on through Microsoft, Google, or OpenID, and LDAP or Active Directory provisioning for automated user lifecycle management. Higher tiers also add account recovery workflows (escrow), tagging for large credential libraries, and options like a downloadable VM appliance for teams that want a turnkey self-hosted deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Passbolt uses a freemium, self-hosted-first pricing model. The Community edition is free forever, open source under AGPLv3, and supports unlimited users with core password management and sharing features, making it accessible to small teams and open-source projects with engineering resources to self-host.

The Pro edition costs EUR 4.50 per user per month billed annually (roughly USD 4.90), requires a minimum of 10 users, and unlocks SSO, LDAP/AD provisioning, and account recovery; volume discounts apply above 100 users. An Enterprise edition adds white-glove migration, custom feature development, and SLA-backed support for organizations with stricter compliance or uptime requirements, priced on request.

features.title: Encrypted credential sharing
features.description: Share passwords and secrets between team members using OpenPGP-based end-to-end encryption without exposing plaintext credentials.

features.title: Private and shared folders
features.description: Organize credentials into folders scoped to individuals, teams, or projects to keep access aligned with need-to-know.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Assign granular read, update, or owner permissions to users and groups for each stored credential.

features.title: Single sign-on and directory sync
features.description: Paid plans support SSO via Microsoft, Google, and OpenID plus LDAP and Active Directory provisioning for automated onboarding and offboarding.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud deployment
features.description: Run Passbolt on your own infrastructure for full data control, or use the Passbolt-hosted cloud edition.

features.title: Activity and audit logging
features.description: Track who accessed or changed which credential and when, supporting internal audits and compliance reviews.

features.title: Open-source, auditable codebase
features.description: The full source code is published under AGPLv3, allowing security teams to independently review and verify the encryption implementation.

pros: Open-source codebase allows independent security audits, unlike closed-source competitors
Free Community edition supports unlimited users with no artificial seat caps
Self-hosting option gives organizations full control over data residency and encryption keys
EU-based company appeals to privacy- and GDPR-focused organizations
Purpose-built for developer and DevOps team workflows with browser and CLI tooling
cons: Self-hosting requires internal DevOps resources to deploy, patch, and maintain the server
Pro edition has a 10-user minimum, which can be costly for very small paid teams
Interface and setup are less consumer-friendly than mainstream password managers like 1Password
Advanced features such as SSO and LDAP are locked behind paid tiers
Smaller company size than larger competitors means a smaller support and partner ecosystem

faqs.question: Is Passbolt really free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community edition is free forever, open source, and supports unlimited users, though it requires self-hosting and lacks SSO and LDAP integrations found in paid tiers.

faqs.question: Can Passbolt be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Passbolt can be deployed on a company's own servers, which is the primary way most Community and many Pro edition customers run it, alongside an optional Passbolt-hosted cloud version.

faqs.question: How much does Passbolt Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Passbolt Pro costs EUR 4.50 per user per month billed annually, with a minimum of 10 users and volume discounts available above 100 users.

faqs.question: Is Passbolt open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Passbolt's full source code is published and distributed under the AGPLv3 license, allowing independent security review.

faqs.question: Who is Passbolt best suited for?
faqs.answer: Passbolt is best suited for software development, IT, and DevOps teams, as well as privacy- or compliance-conscious organizations that want an auditable, self-hostable password manager.

faqs.question: Does Passbolt support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: SSO via Microsoft, Google, and OpenID is available on the paid Pro and Enterprise editions, not the free Community edition.

faqs.question: Where is Passbolt headquartered?
faqs.answer: Passbolt is headquartered in Belvaux, Luxembourg.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Passbolt Community and Pro editions?
faqs.answer: Community is free with unlimited users and core password sharing; Pro adds SSO, LDAP/Active Directory provisioning, tagging, account recovery, and priority support for a per-user monthly fee.

use_cases.audience: Software development and DevOps teams

use_cases.description: Small teams and open-source projects use the free Community edition to replace insecure practices like sharing passwords over chat or spreadsheets.
use_cases.audience: Startups, small teams, and open-source projects

enrichment.short_description: Pave is an AI-native compensation management platform that gives companies real-time market pay data, compensation bands, and merit-cycle workflows to plan and benchmark employee pay.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom (contact sales); free benchmarks available via Market Data Lite for companies with 1-200 employees
enrichment.seo_title: Pave Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pave review 2026: compensation benchmarking and management software pricing, features, pros and cons, and the best alternatives for HR and total rewards teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pave is a compensation management and market-pricing platform founded in 2019 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company has raised more than 175 million dollars in venture funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Bessemer Venture Partners, Craft Ventures, Index Ventures, and Y Combinator, reaching a reported valuation in the billions of dollars, and has grown to roughly 280 employees. Pave's core product connects directly to a company's HRIS to pull real-time compensation data, then benchmarks that data against a dataset sourced from thousands of companies to help HR, People, and total rewards teams price jobs, build pay bands, run merit and equity refresh cycles, and communicate total rewards statements to employees. Its key differentiator versus older, survey-based compensation benchmarking providers like Radford or Mercer is the real-time, always-refreshing nature of its data, since companies feed live HRIS data into the platform rather than relying on annual or semiannual manual surveys; Pave has also layered AI and machine-learning features into the product to power predictive benchmarking, job matching, and compensation planning recommendations. The platform includes a free tier called Market Data Lite, which offers basic salary and equity benchmarks across 250-plus job families for startups with 1 to 200 employees, functioning as a lead-generation and market-education tool; paid tiers such as Market Data Pro extend benchmarking to 150-plus countries and metros and add compensation planning, pay equity analysis, and total rewards communication modules. Pricing is not published and is quoted individually based on company size, modules selected, and data requirements, with reported annual platform fees commonly ranging from roughly 15,000 to 40,000 dollars for companies with 100 to 500 employees, and higher for larger organizations. Pave's target users are HR, People Operations, Total Rewards, and compensation teams at venture-backed startups and scaling technology companies that need defensible, up-to-date pay data to stay competitive in hiring and retention. Its named competitors include Carta Total Compensation, OpenComp, Ravio, and traditional survey providers such as Radford and Mercer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pave is a compensation management platform that helps companies benchmark employee pay against real-time market data, build and maintain compensation bands, and run merit and equity review cycles. Founded in 2019 and based in San Francisco, the company built its product around a direct integration with a customer's HR information system (HRIS), which continuously feeds anonymized compensation data into Pave's dataset rather than relying on periodic manual surveys.

The platform is aimed primarily at HR, People Operations, and total rewards teams at venture-backed and high-growth technology companies, where competitive, defensible pay bands are critical for hiring and retention. Pave has raised substantial venture funding and expanded its dataset to cover a large number of participating companies, positioning it as a leading real-time alternative to legacy compensation survey providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pave's market data and benchmarking tools let teams price open roles and existing employees against live compensation data spanning hundreds of job families and, on paid tiers, 150-plus countries and metros. Compensation band-building tools help HR teams design consistent, leveled pay ranges tied to that market data, while merit cycle and equity refresh workflows let managers propose and approve pay changes in one place.

AI and machine-learning features power predictive job matching (mapping a company's internal roles to external market benchmarks) and generate planning recommendations during cycles. Pave also includes total rewards statement tools that let companies communicate the full value of pay, equity, and benefits to individual employees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pave does not publish standard pricing; access to its core paid platform is quoted individually based on company size, the modules selected (market data, compensation planning, total rewards, and so on), and data requirements. Reported figures suggest companies with 100 to 500 employees commonly pay in the range of 15,000 to 40,000 dollars annually, with organizations of 500 to 1,500 employees typically quoted higher.

Pave offers a free entry point called Market Data Lite for startups with 1 to 200 employees, providing benchmarks across 250-plus job families for the US market plus one additional market of choice, without requiring a paid contract.

features.description: Benchmark pay across 150-plus countries and metros on paid tiers for companies with international teams.

features.description: Uses machine learning to match internal job titles to external market benchmark roles for more accurate pricing.

features.title: Total rewards statements
features.description: Generate personalized statements that communicate the full value of pay, equity, and benefits to employees.

features.title: Free Market Data Lite tier
features.description: Offers basic salary and equity benchmarks across 250-plus job families to startups with 1-200 employees at no cost.

Free Market Data Lite tier gives early-stage startups usable benchmarks without a paid contract

faqs.question: Is Pave free to use?
faqs.answer: Pave offers a free tier called Market Data Lite for startups with 1-200 employees that includes basic salary and equity benchmarks; full platform access is custom-priced.

faqs.question: How much does Pave cost?
faqs.answer: Pave does not publish pricing; it is quoted based on company size and modules, with reported annual fees commonly in the 15,000 to 40,000 dollar range for companies with 100-500 employees.

faqs.question: How does Pave get its compensation data?
faqs.answer: Pave integrates directly with a customer's HRIS to pull real-time compensation data, then aggregates and anonymizes data across thousands of participating companies for benchmarking.

faqs.question: Who uses Pave?
faqs.answer: Pave is used primarily by HR, People Operations, and total rewards teams at venture-backed and high-growth technology companies to benchmark and plan employee pay.

faqs.question: What is Pave's main competitor set?
faqs.answer: Pave competes with Carta Total Compensation, OpenComp, Ravio, and traditional survey-based providers such as Radford and Mercer.

faqs.question: Does Pave support international compensation benchmarking?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pave's Market Data Pro offering benchmarks pay across 150-plus countries and metros on paid plans.

faqs.question: When was Pave founded?
faqs.answer: Pave was founded in 2019 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

enrichment.short_description: Paychex Flex is a cloud-based payroll and human capital management platform from Paychex, a publicly traded (NASDAQ: PAYX) payroll and HR outsourcing provider serving small and midsize businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Paychex Flex Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Paychex Flex review 2026: payroll and HR platform pricing tiers, features, pros and cons, and top alternatives for small and midsize business payroll.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Paychex Flex is the flagship cloud-based payroll and human capital management (HCM) platform of Paychex, Inc., a payroll and HR outsourcing company founded in 1971 by Tom Golisano and headquartered in Rochester, New York. Paychex has traded publicly on NASDAQ under the ticker PAYX since 1983 and has grown into one of the largest payroll and HR services providers in the United States, reporting roughly 19,000 employees worldwide following its April 2025 acquisition of HR and payroll competitor Paycor, which added approximately 2,700 employees and expanded Paychex's presence in the mid-market segment. Paychex Flex itself is the company's unified, browser- and mobile-based platform that consolidates payroll processing, tax filing and compliance, time and attendance, benefits administration, and core HR functions into a single login, replacing older, more fragmented legacy Paychex systems. The platform's key differentiator is the breadth of the parent company's scale and service model: Paychex pairs its software with dedicated payroll specialists, tax compliance guarantees, and an extensive PEO (professional employer organization) and insurance services arm, which pure-software payroll competitors like Gusto or smaller providers cannot match. Pricing is tiered and largely quote-based: the entry-level Flex Essentials plan is the only publicly listed price, starting at 39 dollars per month plus 5 dollars per employee per month for basic payroll and tax filing aimed at businesses with fewer than 20 employees, while Flex Select, Flex Pro, and Flex Enterprise plans add progressively more HR, onboarding, analytics, and dedicated support features and require a custom quote from a sales representative. Paychex Flex targets small businesses through large enterprises, with Flex Essentials and Flex Select aimed at small companies, Flex Pro at growing mid-sized businesses, and Flex Enterprise at larger, more complex workforces needing advanced analytics and 24/7 support. Its primary named competitors include ADP (Workforce Now and RUN by ADP), Gusto, Rippling, and its own recently acquired subsidiary Paycor, alongside QuickBooks Payroll for the smallest-business segment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Paychex Flex is the cloud-based payroll and HR platform of Paychex, Inc., a payroll processing and HR outsourcing company founded in 1971 and headquartered in Rochester, New York. Paychex has been publicly traded on NASDAQ under the ticker PAYX since 1983 and is one of the largest providers of payroll, benefits, and HR services to small and midsize businesses in the United States.

Flex is Paychex's unified platform, replacing older siloed systems with a single browser- and mobile-based login for payroll, tax filing, time and attendance, benefits, and core HR. In April 2025 Paychex completed its acquisition of Paycor, a competing HR and payroll software provider, expanding its headcount and mid-market product footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Paychex Flex handles automated payroll processing and tax filing across federal, state, and local jurisdictions, with compliance guarantees backed by Paychex's tax specialists. Time and attendance tracking, employee self-service, and benefits administration (including access to Paychex's insurance and retirement plan services) are built into the same platform.

Higher tiers add HR advisory support, learning management, applicant tracking and onboarding tools, and analytics dashboards. Because Paychex pairs software with dedicated service teams, customers on most plans can also reach a live payroll specialist rather than relying purely on self-service tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Paychex uses a quote-based pricing model combining a base subscription fee with a per-employee charge, layered with optional add-ons for HR, benefits, and compliance services. The only publicly disclosed starting price is for Flex Essentials, which begins at 39 dollars per month plus 5 dollars per employee per month for basic payroll and tax filing.

The Flex Select, Flex Pro, and Flex Enterprise tiers progressively add HR tools, analytics, and support levels, but require contacting Paychex sales for a custom quote based on company size, state footprint, and selected modules.

features.description: Higher tiers include access to HR professionals for compliance questions and employee relations guidance.

features.description: Lets employees view pay stubs, tax forms, and benefits, and update personal information without contacting HR.

features.description: Provides dashboards on labor costs, turnover, and workforce trends for Flex Pro and Enterprise customers.

Broad ancillary services available, including PEO, insurance, and retirement plan administration
Nationwide tax filing compliance across all US jurisdictions
cons: Pricing beyond the entry-level Flex Essentials plan is not published and requires a sales quote
Customer reviews frequently cite implementation and customer service inconsistency at scale
Can be more expensive than software-only payroll competitors like Gusto for very small teams
Contract terms and add-on fees can make total cost less transparent upfront
Recent Paycor acquisition may mean product changes as the two platforms are integrated

faqs.question: How much does Paychex Flex cost?
faqs.answer: The entry-level Flex Essentials plan starts at 39 dollars per month plus 5 dollars per employee per month; Flex Select, Pro, and Enterprise plans require a custom quote from Paychex sales.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Paychex Flex plans?
faqs.answer: Flex Essentials covers basic payroll and tax filing for small teams, Flex Select adds foundational HR tools, Flex Pro adds features for mid-sized, more complex workforces, and Flex Enterprise adds advanced analytics, automation, and 24/7 support.

faqs.question: Who owns Paychex Flex?
faqs.answer: Paychex Flex is a product of Paychex, Inc., a publicly traded company on NASDAQ under the ticker PAYX, headquartered in Rochester, New York.

faqs.question: Does Paychex Flex handle tax filing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Paychex Flex automatically files federal, state, and local payroll taxes on behalf of employers, backed by compliance guarantees.

faqs.question: Is Paychex Flex suitable for large enterprises?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Flex Enterprise tier is designed for larger organizations with complex workforces, offering advanced analytics and 24/7 support.

faqs.question: What companies compete with Paychex Flex?
faqs.answer: Key competitors include ADP, Gusto, Rippling, and Paycor, which Paychex acquired in April 2025.

faqs.question: Does Paychex Flex include HR support?
faqs.answer: HR advisory support and compliance guidance are included on higher-tier plans (Flex Select and above), not the entry-level Essentials plan.

enrichment.short_description: PayEm is a global spend and procurement management platform that automates purchase requests, corporate card spending, invoice processing, and vendor payments for finance teams.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: PayEm Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PayEm review 2026: spend and procurement management platform pricing, corporate card and AP automation features, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PayEm is a global spend and procurement management platform founded in 2019 by Itamar Jobani, Omer Rimoch, and Yonatan Braude, originally launched out of Tel Aviv, Israel and now headquartered in New York with additional offices internationally. The company emerged from stealth in 2022 with 27 million dollars in funding and has since raised additional equity and credit facilities, including a reported combination of 20 million dollars in equity and 200 million dollars in credit financing from investors such as Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group, Viola Credit, and Collaborative Fund, bringing total funding to roughly 247 million dollars. PayEm's product automates the full procure-to-pay lifecycle for finance and procurement teams: purchase requests, approval workflows, virtual and physical corporate cards, invoice processing (including OCR-based data capture), ACH and international vendor payments, and automatic reconciliation back to the general ledger, all built around configurable, multi-step approval rules. Its key differentiator versus point solutions is breadth: rather than requiring separate tools for corporate cards, accounts payable automation, and purchase requisitioning, PayEm combines all three in a single platform with native multi-entity and multi-currency support, which appeals particularly to companies with international operations or multiple subsidiaries. Pricing is not published; PayEm quotes customers individually based on company size, transaction volume, and modules used, with tiers commonly described informally as ranging from a basic plan for very small businesses up to Pro and Enterprise tiers with deeper automation, integrations, and dedicated support. PayEm's target customers are lean finance and procurement teams, often at mid-market and internationally operating companies, that need tighter spend control and visibility without adding headcount. Named competitors in the spend management and procurement automation space include Ramp, Brex, Airbase, and Coupa, with Payhawk and Spendesk as additional points of comparison in the European market.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PayEm is a spend and procurement management platform that automates the request-to-reconciliation process for finance teams, combining purchase requisitioning, corporate cards, invoice processing, and vendor payments in one system. The company was founded in 2019 and emerged from stealth in 2022 with a 27 million dollar funding round, having originally launched out of Tel Aviv, Israel before establishing headquarters in New York.

PayEm is built for lean finance and procurement teams that need centralized visibility and control over company spend, particularly organizations with multiple entities, currencies, or international operations, where PayEm's multi-currency and multi-entity support is a core differentiator versus single-country spend tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: PayEm's procurement tools let employees submit purchase and budget requests that route through configurable, multi-step approval workflows before spend is authorized. Virtual and physical corporate cards can be issued with merchant restrictions and spend limits, with automatic receipt capture tied to each transaction.

On the accounts payable side, PayEm uses OCR-based invoice processing to extract line-item data automatically, then routes invoices for approval and payment via ACH or international rails. Reconciliation tools automatically match card and invoice transactions back to the general ledger, reducing manual close-the-books work for finance teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PayEm does not publish pricing on its website; access is quoted individually based on company size, transaction volume, and which modules (procurement, cards, AP automation) a customer needs. This subscription-based, custom-quoted model is typical of enterprise spend management software.

Third-party sources describe PayEm's offering as spanning a basic tier for very small businesses, a Pro tier with more advanced automation and integrations, and an Enterprise tier with custom solutions, dedicated support, and advanced security features, though exact pricing for each requires contacting PayEm sales.

features.title: Purchase request workflows

features.description: Matches card and invoice transactions back to the general ledger automatically, reducing manual close work.

features.title: ACH and international vendor payments
features.description: Processes domestic ACH and cross-border vendor payments directly from the platform.

features.title: Configurable approval policies
features.description: Set custom, role-based approval rules for purchase requests, card spend, and invoice payments.

Strong multi-entity and multi-currency support for internationally operating companies
OCR-based invoice capture reduces manual data entry for accounts payable teams
Configurable approval workflows adapt to different company spend policies
Backed by substantial funding, including large credit facilities to support card issuance
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation to obtain a quote
Smaller brand recognition than larger competitors like Ramp, Brex, or Coupa
Breadth of features can mean a steeper learning curve for very small teams
Company has changed headquarters location over its history, which may signal organizational flux
Best value likely requires adopting multiple modules (cards, AP, procurement) rather than one in isolation

faqs.question: How much does PayEm cost?
faqs.answer: PayEm does not publish pricing; costs are quoted individually based on company size, transaction volume, and which modules (procurement, cards, AP automation) are used.

faqs.question: What does PayEm do?
faqs.answer: PayEm is a spend and procurement management platform that automates purchase requests, corporate card spending, invoice processing, and vendor payments for finance teams.

faqs.question: When was PayEm founded?
faqs.answer: PayEm was founded in 2019 and emerged from stealth mode in 2022 with a 27 million dollar funding round.

faqs.question: Does PayEm support multiple currencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, PayEm supports multi-entity and multi-currency spend management, which is useful for companies with international operations.

faqs.question: Who are PayEm's main competitors?
faqs.answer: PayEm competes with Ramp, Brex, Airbase, and Coupa in spend and procurement management, with Payhawk and Spendesk as additional comparisons in Europe.

faqs.question: Does PayEm issue corporate cards?
faqs.answer: Yes, PayEm issues virtual and physical corporate cards with merchant restrictions, spend limits, and automatic receipt capture.

faqs.question: Who is PayEm designed for?
faqs.answer: PayEm is designed for lean finance and procurement teams, particularly at mid-market and internationally operating companies needing tighter spend control.

enrichment.short_description: Payhawk is a European spend management platform combining corporate cards, expense management, accounts payable, and procurement automation with AI-driven controls for mid-market and enterprise companies.
enrichment.starting_price: $599/month (Cards and Expenses or Bill Payments plan)
enrichment.headquarters: London, United Kingdom (founded in Sofia, Bulgaria)
enrichment.seo_title: Payhawk Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Payhawk review 2026: spend management and corporate card platform pricing, features, pros and cons, and top alternatives for finance teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Payhawk is a spend management platform founded in 2018 in Sofia, Bulgaria by Hristo Borisov, Boyko Karadzhov, and Konstantin Dzhengozov, and is now registered and headquartered in London, United Kingdom, with additional offices across Europe and the United States. In March 2022 Payhawk raised a 100 million dollar Series B extension led by Lightspeed Venture Partners at a valuation of roughly 1 billion dollars, becoming the first Bulgarian company to reach unicorn status; total funding to date is roughly 239 million dollars from investors including Greenoaks, Sprints Capital, HubSpot Ventures, and QED Investors, and the company reported plans in early 2026 to raise further funding at a valuation near 2 billion dollars. Payhawk's platform combines corporate Visa cards issued across multiple currencies, expense management with automatic receipt capture, invoice and bill payment processing, and procurement (procure-to-pay) workflows, layered with AI-powered automation for approvals, policy enforcement, and reconciliation. Its key differentiator versus US-centric competitors like Ramp or Brex is its European-first design: multi-currency cards that avoid foreign exchange fees within eight supported currencies, compliance with European accounting and tax requirements, and a customer base spanning 32-plus countries, making it a common choice for mid-market and enterprise companies headquartered in or operating across Europe. Pricing is not fully published but is known to start at 599 dollars per month for the Cards and Expenses or Bill Payments plan and 899 dollars per month for the Procure to Pay plan, with Payhawk pricing based on the number of admin and accountant seats and selected features rather than transaction volume or employee count, meaning every paid plan includes unlimited employee cardholders and transactions. Payhawk's target users are finance teams at mid-market and enterprise companies, particularly those with multi-entity or multi-country European operations, seeking a single system for cards, expenses, invoices, and procurement in place of multiple point solutions. Named competitors include Ramp, Brex, Airbase, Spendesk, and PayEm.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Payhawk is a spend management platform that combines corporate cards, expense management, accounts payable, and procurement automation in a single system for finance teams. The company was founded in 2018 in Sofia, Bulgaria, and is now headquartered in London, United Kingdom, becoming the first Bulgarian company to reach unicorn status after a 100 million dollar Series B extension in 2022 valued it at roughly 1 billion dollars.

Payhawk is designed with a European-first approach, issuing multi-currency corporate cards, supporting compliance with regional accounting and tax rules, and serving mid-market and enterprise customers across 32-plus countries, positioning it as a strong alternative to US-centric spend management platforms for companies with European operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Payhawk issues Visa corporate cards across seven currencies, letting cardholders avoid foreign exchange fees when spending in one of eight supported currencies, with a competitive markup applied for other currencies. Expense management tools automatically capture receipts and reconcile card transactions, while spend policies let admins set monthly limits, ATM withdrawal rules, and merchant restrictions per employee or team.

Beyond cards, Payhawk handles invoice and bill payment processing and procure-to-pay workflows for purchase requests and vendor management, with AI-powered automation applied across approvals and reconciliation. The platform integrates with major accounting systems to keep books synchronized automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Payhawk's published starting prices are 599 dollars per month for its Cards and Expenses or Bill Payments plan, and 899 dollars per month for its Procure to Pay plan. Pricing is based on the number of admin and accountant seats and the features selected, not on transaction volume or the number of employees, so every paid plan includes unlimited employee cardholders and card transactions.
Businesses with more complex needs, higher seat counts, or additional add-ons can request a tailored quote from Payhawk's sales team based on team size and operational requirements.

features.description: Uses AI automation to speed up approval routing and match transactions to accounting records.

Unlimited employee cardholders and transactions included on every paid plan regardless of seat pricing
Combines cards, expenses, invoices, and procurement in one platform for finance teams
Backed by significant funding and unicorn valuation, supporting continued product investment
Serves customers across 32-plus countries with region-specific compliance support
cons: Starting price of 599 dollars per month is relatively high for very small businesses
Full pricing detail requires a custom quote for larger seat counts or add-ons
Primarily positioned for mid-market and enterprise customers rather than early-stage startups
Currency markup of 1.99 percent applies outside the eight fee-free supported currencies
Less US market presence and brand recognition than competitors like Ramp or Brex

faqs.question: How much does Payhawk cost?
faqs.answer: Payhawk starts at 599 dollars per month for its Cards and Expenses or Bill Payments plan, and 899 dollars per month for its Procure to Pay plan, priced by admin seats rather than transaction volume.

faqs.question: Where is Payhawk headquartered?
faqs.answer: Payhawk is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, having been founded in 2018 in Sofia, Bulgaria.

faqs.question: Does Payhawk charge foreign exchange fees?
faqs.answer: Payhawk avoids FX fees when a card is issued and used in the same currency, for eight supported currencies; a 1.99 percent markup applies to other currencies.

faqs.question: Is Payhawk a unicorn company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Payhawk became the first Bulgarian unicorn in March 2022 after raising a 100 million dollar Series B extension at a roughly 1 billion dollar valuation.

faqs.question: What does Payhawk's Procure to Pay plan include?
faqs.answer: The Procure to Pay plan adds purchase request and vendor procurement workflows on top of Payhawk's core cards, expenses, and bill payment features.

faqs.question: Who are Payhawk's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Payhawk competes with Ramp, Brex, Airbase, Spendesk, and PayEm in the spend management space.

faqs.question: Does Payhawk limit the number of employee cardholders?
faqs.answer: No, every paid Payhawk plan includes unlimited employee seats and card transactions; pricing is based on admin and accountant seats instead.

enrichment.short_description: Payload CMS is an open-source, TypeScript-native headless content management system and application framework that developers self-host or run on Payload Cloud, now owned by Figma.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, open source); Payload Cloud plans from $35/month
enrichment.seo_title: Payload CMS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Payload CMS review 2026: open-source TypeScript headless CMS pricing, self-hosted vs cloud options, features, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Payload CMS is an open-source, TypeScript-native headless content management system and application framework founded in 2018 by James Mikrut, Elliot DeNolf, and Dan Ribbens, three full-stack developers frustrated with existing CMS options that deprioritized developer experience. The company is headquartered in Grand Rapids, Michigan, bootstrapped for several years before joining the Y Combinator accelerator in 2022 and raising 5.2 million dollars in seed funding from investors including Gradient Ventures and MongoDB Ventures; in 2025 Payload was acquired by design software company Figma, which now owns and continues to develop the product. Payload's key differentiator versus other headless CMS platforms like Contentful, Sanity, or Strapi is that it is code-first and fully open source: developers define content models, access control, and hooks directly in TypeScript, and the entire admin panel is generated automatically from that schema, with the codebase self-hostable on any Node.js-compatible infrastructure using a Postgres or MongoDB database. Beyond content management, Payload doubles as a general-purpose application framework, letting developers build authentication, e-commerce logic, and custom business workflows on the same backend rather than bolting a CMS onto a separate application. Pricing follows an open-source-first model: the self-hosted software is free forever, and organizations pay only for their own hosting infrastructure; for teams that prefer a managed option, Payload Cloud offers paid tiers, with a Standard plan around 35 dollars per month for smaller projects and a Pro plan around 199 dollars per month for higher-traffic, dedicated-cluster deployments, plus a custom-priced Enterprise Cloud tier starting near 10,000 dollars per year for annual commitments. Payload's target users are software development teams and technical product teams, particularly those building content-heavy websites, e-commerce storefronts, or internal tools who want full control of their stack and data without vendor lock-in. Named competitors include Contentful, Sanity, Strapi (another open-source, self-hostable option), and Directus.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Payload CMS is an open-source, TypeScript-native headless content management system and application framework, founded in 2018 by developers James Mikrut, Elliot DeNolf, and Dan Ribbens in Grand Rapids, Michigan. The project was built as a code-first alternative to existing headless CMS platforms, letting developers define content models, relationships, and access control directly in TypeScript rather than through a proprietary visual schema builder.

After bootstrapping for several years, Payload joined Y Combinator in 2022 and raised seed funding from investors including Gradient Ventures and MongoDB Ventures. In 2025 the company was acquired by Figma, which now owns and continues to develop the product alongside Figma's broader design and development tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Payload's admin panel is generated automatically from a developer's TypeScript configuration, covering collections, fields, and relationships without manual UI work. Built-in access control lets teams define field-level and document-level permissions in code, and lifecycle hooks let developers run custom logic on create, update, or delete events.

Because Payload doubles as an application framework rather than a pure content repository, teams can build authentication, e-commerce, and custom business logic on the same Node.js backend. The software supports both Postgres and MongoDB as a database layer and can be self-hosted on any Node-compatible infrastructure, or deployed via the managed Payload Cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Payload CMS itself is free and open source; teams that self-host pay only for their own server, database, and storage infrastructure, which can range from a few dollars per month for small projects to hundreds of dollars for higher-traffic deployments depending on the hosting provider.

For teams that prefer a managed option, Payload Cloud offers a Standard plan around 35 dollars per month with modest compute, database, and storage allowances, and a Pro plan around 199 dollars per month with a dedicated cluster and higher-availability compute. An Enterprise Cloud tier is available on request, reportedly starting near 10,000 dollars per year for annual commitments, with usage-based overage charges for storage and bandwidth on paid plans.

features.title: Code-first content modeling
features.description: Define collections, fields, and relationships directly in TypeScript, with the admin UI generated automatically from that schema.

features.title: Self-hosted or Payload Cloud deployment
features.description: Run the fully open-source software on your own infrastructure, or use Payload's managed cloud hosting for a turnkey setup.

features.title: Granular access control
features.description: Define field-level and document-level permissions in code to control exactly what each user or role can see and edit.

features.title: Lifecycle hooks
features.description: Run custom TypeScript logic automatically on content create, update, delete, and other events.

features.title: Postgres and MongoDB support
features.description: Choose between relational (Postgres) or document (MongoDB) databases depending on project needs.

features.title: Application framework capabilities
features.description: Build authentication, e-commerce, and custom business logic on the same backend rather than a separate application layer.

features.title: REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Auto-generated APIs let front-end applications and third-party systems consume content programmatically.

pros: Fully open source with no cost for the software itself, only hosting
Code-first, TypeScript-native approach appeals strongly to developer-led teams
Doubles as an application framework, reducing the need for a separate backend
Self-hosting option avoids vendor lock-in and gives full data control
Now backed by Figma's resources following the 2025 acquisition
cons: Requires TypeScript and Node.js development skills, less accessible to non-technical content teams
Self-hosting requires ongoing infrastructure management and maintenance
Managed Payload Cloud pricing can add up quickly at the Pro and Enterprise tiers
Smaller ecosystem and plugin marketplace than more established CMS platforms like Contentful
Future product direction is less certain following the Figma acquisition

faqs.question: Is Payload CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Payload CMS is free and open source when self-hosted; you pay only for your own hosting infrastructure. Payload Cloud offers paid managed hosting starting around 35 dollars per month.

faqs.question: What programming language does Payload CMS use?
faqs.answer: Payload CMS is built with TypeScript and Node.js, and content models are defined directly in TypeScript code.

faqs.question: Can Payload CMS be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Payload is open source and can be self-hosted on any Node.js-compatible infrastructure with a Postgres or MongoDB database.

faqs.question: Who owns Payload CMS?
faqs.answer: Payload CMS was acquired by design software company Figma in 2025, after being founded in 2018 in Grand Rapids, Michigan.

faqs.question: Is Payload CMS just a content management system?
faqs.answer: No, Payload also functions as a general application framework, letting developers build authentication, e-commerce, and custom business logic on the same backend.

faqs.question: How much does Payload Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Payload Cloud's Standard plan is around 35 dollars per month and its Pro plan is around 199 dollars per month, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier for larger deployments.

faqs.question: What are Payload CMS's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Payload competes with other headless CMS platforms including Contentful, Sanity, Strapi, and Directus.

enrichment.short_description: Paylocity is a publicly traded (NASDAQ: PCTY) cloud-based payroll and human capital management (HCM) platform serving mid-sized organizations across payroll, benefits, talent, and workforce management.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom, quote-based (third-party estimates suggest plans from roughly $20/employee/month)
enrichment.seo_title: Paylocity Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Paylocity review 2026: cloud payroll and HCM platform pricing, features, pros and cons, and top alternatives for mid-sized business HR teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Paylocity is a cloud-based payroll and human capital management (HCM) platform provided by Paylocity Holding Corporation, founded in 1997 and headquartered in Schaumburg, Illinois. The company has been publicly traded on NASDAQ under the ticker PCTY since its 2014 initial public offering, and reports roughly 6,700 employees serving tens of thousands of client organizations across the United States. Paylocity's platform unifies payroll processing, benefits administration, time and attendance, talent management (recruiting, onboarding, performance, and learning), and workforce communication tools, differentiating itself from larger legacy providers like ADP through a modern, mobile-first user interface and social-collaboration-style employee engagement features such as its Community feed and peer recognition tools, aimed at improving adoption among younger, distributed workforces. The company primarily targets mid-market organizations, generally companies with 50 to 5,000 employees, positioning itself between simpler small-business payroll tools like Gusto and enterprise-scale suites like Workday or ADP Workforce Now. Paylocity's pricing is not published and is quoted individually per employee per month (PEPM) based on company size, selected modules, and contract term; third-party industry estimates place core payroll-and-HR pricing around 18 to 22 dollars PEPM, with a full HCM suite spanning payroll, benefits, time, talent, and learning more typically running 26 to 33 dollars PEPM for mid-market customers, alongside separate implementation fees commonly estimated at 10 to 20 percent of first-year software costs. Paylocity's target users are HR, payroll, and finance leaders at mid-sized companies who need an integrated HCM system without the complexity and cost of enterprise suites designed for very large organizations. The platform also includes workforce analytics and reporting dashboards that help HR and finance leaders track labor costs, headcount trends, and turnover in real time, and it offers a library of pre-built integrations plus an open API for connecting to accounting, benefits carrier, and expense management systems. Implementation typically involves a guided onboarding process handled by a dedicated Paylocity implementation team, with additional fees layered on top of the subscription cost, and the company has continued to invest in AI-assisted features such as automated compliance alerts and payroll error detection to reduce manual review work for HR teams. Its primary named competitors include ADP, Paycor, UKG (Ultimate Kronos Group), Paychex, and Workday for larger enterprise customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Paylocity is a cloud-based payroll and human capital management (HCM) platform provided by Paylocity Holding Corporation, founded in 1997 and headquartered in Schaumburg, Illinois. The company has been publicly traded on NASDAQ under the ticker PCTY since 2014 and has grown into one of the larger independent HCM software providers focused on the mid-market segment.

The platform is built around a modern, mobile-first interface and social-collaboration-style engagement tools, differentiating it from older-generation payroll and HR systems. Paylocity primarily serves mid-sized organizations, typically companies with 50 to 5,000 employees, that need an integrated system spanning payroll, benefits, time, and talent management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Paylocity's core modules cover payroll processing and tax filing, benefits administration and open enrollment, time and attendance tracking, and talent management including recruiting, onboarding, performance reviews, and learning management, all unified in one system with shared employee data.

The platform's Community feature functions like an internal social feed for peer recognition and company announcements, aimed at improving employee engagement and adoption, particularly among frontline and distributed teams. Paylocity also offers workforce analytics and reporting dashboards to help HR and finance leaders track labor costs and turnover trends.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Paylocity does not publish standard pricing; the company quotes customers individually using a per-employee-per-month (PEPM) model based on company size, selected modules, and contract length. Industry estimates suggest core payroll-and-HR-only pricing generally falls around 18 to 22 dollars PEPM.

A full HCM suite covering payroll, benefits, time, talent, and learning more typically runs 26 to 33 dollars PEPM for mid-market customers, with implementation fees commonly estimated at 10 to 20 percent of first-year software costs and additional charges for premium support or specialized modules.

faqs.answer: Paylocity covers payroll, benefits administration, time and attendance, and talent management including recruiting, onboarding, performance, and learning.

faqs.answer: Paylocity is headquartered in Schaumburg, Illinois, and was founded in 1997.

faqs.answer: Paylocity competes with ADP, Paycor, UKG, Paychex, and Workday for larger enterprise customers.

enrichment.short_description: Paymo is a work management application combining project management, time tracking, task management, and invoicing, built for small teams, agencies, and freelancers.
enrichment.starting_price: Free; paid plans from $5.90/user/month billed annually
enrichment.seo_title: Paymo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Paymo review 2026: project management, time tracking, and invoicing software pricing, features, pros and cons, and top alternatives for small teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Paymo is a work management application that combines project management, time tracking, task management, and client invoicing in one platform, founded in 2008 by Jan Lukacs and Andrei Gadoiu and headquartered in Oradea, Romania. The company has grown into a lean, largely bootstrapped software business with a small team spread across multiple continents, reportedly generating several million dollars in annual revenue from thousands of paying customers as of recent estimates. Paymo's key differentiator versus larger project management suites like Asana, Monday.com, or ClickUp is that it bundles native time tracking and client invoicing directly into the project workflow, so freelancers, agencies, and small businesses can track billable hours against tasks and generate invoices from that tracked time without connecting a separate app like Harvest or FreshBooks. The platform supports multiple project views including Kanban boards, Gantt-style timelines, task lists, and a table/spreadsheet view, along with resource scheduling, leave management on higher tiers, and integrations with tools like Google Workspace, Slack, and Zapier, plus native desktop and mobile time-tracking apps with an idle-time detection feature. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free plan supports a single user with limited clients and projects, while paid plans scale from a Solo tier for individual freelancers, through a Plus tier aimed at growing teams with unlimited users, up to a Pro tier adding task dependencies, portfolio Gantt charts, employee scheduling, and priority support; paid plans are billed per user per month, with an annual billing discount versus monthly pricing. Paymo's target users are freelancers, small creative and marketing agencies, and small to midsize businesses that want project management, time tracking, and invoicing consolidated into one affordable tool rather than paying for and integrating multiple separate SaaS products. Named competitors include Asana, Monday.com, ClickUp, Toggl Track (for time tracking specifically), and Harvest (for time tracking and invoicing).

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Paymo is a work management application that combines project management, time tracking, task management, and client invoicing in a single platform. The company was co-founded in 2008 by Jan Lukacs and Andrei Gadoiu and is headquartered in Oradea, Romania, operating as a small, largely bootstrapped team serving small businesses, agencies, and freelancers.

The platform's core positioning is consolidation: rather than requiring separate tools for task management, time tracking, and invoicing, Paymo bundles all three so that tracked billable hours flow directly into client invoices. This appeals particularly to service-based businesses like creative and marketing agencies that need to track time against specific projects and bill clients accordingly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Paymo supports multiple project views, including Kanban-style boards, Gantt-style timeline charts, simple task lists, and a spreadsheet-style table view, letting teams work the way that suits them. Native time tracking is available via desktop, mobile, and browser apps, including automatic idle-time detection so tracked hours stay accurate.

Tracked time flows directly into Paymo's invoicing module, letting freelancers and agencies generate client invoices from billable hours without a separate invoicing tool. Higher tiers add resource and employee scheduling, leave management, task dependencies, and portfolio-level Gantt charts for managing multiple projects at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Paymo offers a free plan limited to a single user with a small number of clients and projects, unlimited time tracking, and basic invoicing. Paid plans start with Solo at 5.90 dollars per month for a single user, followed by Plus at 10.90 dollars per user per month and Pro at 16.90 dollars per user per month, both supporting unlimited users, with annual billing offering a discount versus month-to-month pricing.

The Plus plan adds features like spreadsheet views, recurring tasks, timesheet reports, and project templates, while Pro adds task dependencies, portfolio Gantt charts, employee scheduling, leave management, and priority support, making it the best fit for growing agencies managing multiple simultaneous projects.

features.title: Multiple project views
features.description: Switch between Kanban boards, Gantt-style timelines, task lists, and a spreadsheet-style table view depending on the project.

features.title: Native time tracking
features.description: Track billable hours via desktop, mobile, and browser apps with automatic idle-time detection.

features.title: Time-to-invoice workflow
features.description: Generate client invoices directly from tracked billable time without a separate invoicing tool.

features.title: Resource and employee scheduling
features.description: Plan team workload and availability, with leave management available on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Task dependencies and portfolio Gantt charts
features.description: Map relationships between tasks and view multiple projects together on the Pro plan.

features.title: Project templates
features.description: Reuse project structures and task lists for recurring client work to speed up setup.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connects with tools like Google Workspace, Slack, and Zapier to fit into existing workflows.

pros: Combines project management, time tracking, and invoicing in one affordable tool
Free plan is usable for solo freelancers with light client and project needs
Multiple project views (Kanban, Gantt, table, list) suit different working styles
Time tracking flows directly into invoicing without needing a separate app
Priced significantly lower than larger project management suites like Asana or Monday.com
cons: Free plan is limited to a single user and very few clients or projects
Advanced features like task dependencies and resource scheduling require the top Pro tier
Smaller company and team size than larger competitors, which can mean a smaller support team
Interface can feel dense for teams that only need simple task management
Fewer native integrations than larger, more established project management platforms

faqs.question: Is Paymo free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Paymo offers a free plan for a single user with limited clients, projects, and storage; paid plans start at 5.90 dollars per month for the Solo tier.

faqs.question: How much does Paymo cost?
faqs.answer: Paymo's paid plans start at Solo for 5.90 dollars per month (1 user), Plus at 10.90 dollars per user per month, and Pro at 16.90 dollars per user per month, all billed annually for these rates.

faqs.question: Does Paymo include invoicing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Paymo lets users generate client invoices directly from tracked billable time, without needing a separate invoicing tool.

faqs.question: Where is Paymo based?
faqs.answer: Paymo is headquartered in Oradea, Romania, and was founded in 2008.

faqs.question: Who is Paymo best suited for?
faqs.answer: Paymo is best suited for freelancers, small creative and marketing agencies, and small to midsize businesses that want project management, time tracking, and invoicing in one tool.

faqs.question: Does Paymo track idle time?
faqs.answer: Yes, Paymo's desktop and mobile time-tracking apps include automatic idle-time detection to keep tracked hours accurate.

faqs.question: What are Paymo's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Paymo competes with Asana, Monday.com, and ClickUp for project management, and with Toggl Track and Harvest for time tracking and invoicing specifically.

faqs.question: Does Paymo support team scheduling?
faqs.answer: Yes, resource and employee scheduling is available, with leave management included on the Pro plan.

use_cases.title: Freelancer time tracking and invoicing
use_cases.description: Independent freelancers use Paymo's Solo plan to track billable hours against client projects and generate invoices directly from that tracked time.
use_cases.audience: Freelancers and independent contractors

use_cases.title: Managing agency client projects
use_cases.description: Small creative and marketing agencies use Paymo's Kanban and Gantt views alongside time tracking to manage multiple simultaneous client projects and bill accurately.
use_cases.audience: Small creative and marketing agencies

use_cases.title: Coordinating small business team workload
use_cases.description: Small businesses use Paymo's resource scheduling and task management tools to coordinate team workload and deadlines across projects.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and team leads
